sentences.text: With only little brother, father, and mother.
And I don't know.
I heard rumors that she died in a brothel.
But I'm not sure.
That's rumors.
But the father, mother, and little boy, we never knew.
I mean, probably they're in lists somewhere, but I don't know.
Well, she was about my age.
It was in '41, she was, well-- '41, how old was in '41, oh, 18.
Two, two other people by the-- you know, Jewish people I spoke to later on.
I never, I never checked.
My husband tried, but it was just at the end of the war, where the lists were not really made.
And that was that.
So but I know that she never went to Venezuela, that she was arrested.
And people like that who were arrested, that was that.
Who were the informers?
I don't know.
People who wanted their apartment.
Or people who wanted to get some money from the commandant, too.
And in that, I should open a parentheses.
I was going to speak about it later, but I, I'll speak now.
It's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had in church, I, I knew a, an older woman, who had a large apartment.
And the German put their officers with civilians.
The idea is that if they put them all together, a bomb could blow them up.
But if they are spread through the population or in schools or things like that, they would be safer.
And she told me my, my German official is very nice, but he screams.
Every morning, he screams.
I think he's crazy.
And I happened to meet that man at the University later on.
He spoke very good French.
His name was Wolf Schieck, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wolfgang Schieck.
S-C-H-E-I-- no.
I, I mistake my vowel.
S-H-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] S-C-H-I-E-C-K.
And he was a professor of French in Germany.
And he was a translator for the commandant tour.
And he had to translate the letters of denunciation that French people sent against other people.
That is a shameful thing.
To this day, I refuse to read anything that is not signed.
And it landed on his desk.
And he always complained being too late, being late.
And he gave himself 24 hours to have the people told that the Gestapo was coming to them.
And he saved a number of people.
And I think he was recognized by Jewish organization.
He risked not only his life, but his wife's life and his daughter's life.
I know him, sure.
I went to see him.
My first sabbatical, I went to see him in Gottingen.
He yelled.
I asked him why he yelled.
And he said he yelled because when he woke up and he saw his uniform, he could do nothing else but scream.
He was a Lutheran but of the good sort, obviously.
And then he told me-- since he knew me pretty well, he said, why don't you go see such and such and tell them that they be careful, that the police will come tomorrow.
So I did a few of those missions.
People who were Jewish, and some who were not.
And finally, I told my own father.
Because we were hiding my uncle, the mayor of Bellegarde, the Germans were pursuing.
He was at our house.
And our maid had told that we are hiding somebody.
And my-- our friend, my-- Schieck, told me get rid of your uncle.
[LAUGHS] I got rid of-- we got rid of the uncle.
They came, and nothing happened of course.
Of course, we fired the maid.
I don't have to tell you.
[LAUGHS] But you see, that you had Germans like that.
And they risked-- I don't know what they risked, but they risked a lot.
Of course, many people who were told didn't believe it.
From, from the beginning.
I mean, when I went to see the Tradelius, it was to lift their morale.
When we tried to-- well, it was-- my parents were like that.
You had to help when people were in need.
Grapevine, you could say.
Nothing in return, just why don't you.
And that's how it happened that a friend-- we made the Tradelius probably.
He said, you know, why don't you go buy, buy cookies from the Oppenheimers?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Oppenheimers had a house-- an apartment in a very modest house, not too far from us.
And they were lawyers, he was a lawyer.
They were rich.
They were the one with the children I mentioned.
And since he was a lawyer, he could do nothing with his law.
The money had been confiscated in Germany.
He had his furniture, he sold piece by piece.
Finally, Mrs.
Oppenheimer baked cookies and kugelhopf and those German-Jewish pastries.
And me and my friend, the people I knew, said you need cookies, go to buy from the Oppenheimers.
And that's how they, they made a little money to survive.
And that's how I got to know them and talk to them and visit them, even when I don't need any cookies.
Just to talk to them, to tell them that we love them and so on.
What else could I give, huh?
And then the old man, the old father Oppenheimer, the old grandfather-- he looked like a white rabbit.
I say white rabbit because he had white hair, and he always had a problem with his eyes that were red.
He was a little, tiny man.
And he had to go shopping in the morning for food.
And he had his yellow star.
So we used to hold on together so his yellow star wouldn't show.
The only problem it is that his coupons had a big J on it.
So by the fact that he had a big J, he couldn't go to a store before 11:00 in the morning.
We stood in line at 5:00 in the morning.
By the time 11:00 came, it was nothing left except a few onions.
And the grocer could not accept those tickets because of the J.
If I took his tickets, it wouldn't be accepted.
But the grocery man was a nice man, so we managed to smuggle a few things.
And then two people I knew at my church, we got false coupon, without the J, so they could at least feed themselves, you know.
They had to buy flour at the black market, sugar at the black market, to make their cookies.
But they had to need potatoes and other things and meat.
So you see, that's how it was.
But then they left Dijon.
And they went in the Alps near Aix-les-Bains.
I don't know, but they sent me a note--
They, they were able to get out because--
No.
At the time, it was where the demarcation line-- and it was after Pearl Harbor, and the Germans occupied all of France, so you could rarely travel.
They couldn't travel with much, a few-- a few trunks.
No furniture, they had to get rid of it.
People are always are willing to buy for nothing, you know.
They-- it's terrible.
I mean, people are bird of prey.
But anyway, they went there.
And my friend Tradelius remain in the south.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had a son, whose name was Heinz.
And they gave him most of the money they had, for him to go to Johannesburg.
You know, you had to pay big caution, and they felt maybe they could join him.
And Annalise, the daughter, married a Spaniard, in order to have a Spanish name.
And she-- he was handsome, she was frivolous.
It came to nothing.
She abandoned her kids.
She was not a nice person.
But that's beside the point.
So remained the--
In the-- no, no.
Near the, near the Pyrenees, where they were.
But the father and the mother were incarcerated in the camp of Gurs.
Maybe you have seen the name.
It's written J-U-R-S.
It was a concentration camp, but they didn't kill people.
They took people from there to Germany.
So my father went there.
It was difficult, because the trains were difficult.
Everything was difficult from Dijon.
Why did he go?
Because he loved them.
And to take for them, you would laugh-- take money, of course-- but take threads and needles.
It was forbidden in that camp.
So people who lost their button and all that or their clothes, you know, they were in rags in no time.
So he brought them that.
My mother had prepared something.
And then it was arranged that they would escape the camp and go over the border to Spain, and then, of course, Portugal, and then maybe South Africa.
The, the son could send them money in Madrid or Lisbon.
They had addresses.
I mean, those things could be arranged.
So they fled.
And his wife, Mrs.
Tradelius, who had been the Jewish princess, the frivolous woman, somewhat obese, refuse to walk.
They were halfway, almost over the border, she said no, I don't go a step further.
And he, being a good husband, he remained.
They went back, he was caught.
He-- she was hiding with her daughter under that Spanish name.
They were left alone.
He was caught.
And before he was taken, he sent me a little card.
He said to me, keep the piano and the statue-- the statue I have upstairs, a little statue that I liked.
Because it was left in a-- some place, a safe place in, in Dijon.
The piano is in Ohio now.
[LAUGHS] You know, we-- I never part with those things, because to me, they mean so much.
And he died, of course, in Auschwitz.
He was-- he felt protected because he had had the Iron Cross.
During the First World War, he had been a captain or he had [INAUDIBLE] even.
He felt I had done my service.
They are going to, to take me away, but I'll come back.
Yes.
It was very unusual, but nothing bothered.
I don't think it was noticed.
He, he just went.
Well, not except visiting them and being, you know.
Not personally, because I was-- I was young, and I was not in the FFI.
I was not in the-- my parents would have absolutely forbade it.
They felt we had-- our job was to get the studies and get out of it and help wherever we could.
And besides, it was not good form for a woman to go with men in, in things like that.
It would, no.
Yes, but not openly.
For instance, I, I was told take five false identity cards to such farm.
I had to go with my bike, because we have no other transportation.
Imagine, there would have been a barrage of police.
They would have looked through my paper.
They would have seen the five identification cards.
I would have been arrested, of course.
I-- I took them many times.
Some-- a man gave them to me or a woman in our church or somewhere else, a friend of a friend.
And say, you take them to that farm in that village.
And I did.
And I came back with a few potatoes, and that was the justification.
If I was-- where are you going, I'm going to try to get some food.
I come back with my potatoes.
It was dangerous, but that's how it was done.
Yes, but I always managed.
Like I was carrying a newspaper called which was a big-- big newspaper of the [FRENCH].
It was Catholic, Protestant with everything.
And much involved--
[FRENCH].
[FRENCH].
[FRENCH].
[FRENCH]
I will spell it out for you.
It was a very strong thing.
And you had to carry the, the, the newspaper.
But we had no car, so put it on the bike with wrapping.
But if you fall and it spills out, which it did, [LAUGHS] well, you have to gather it.
People help you, they see what it is.
And somebody might say something.
But they didn't.
You know, little things like that.
No, no, never was caught.
I was questioned.
I was caught passing letters at the border, when we had the two-- the two zones.
And the Germans said I was a foolish kid.
I shouldn't do such a thing.
They were correspondence.
You were forbidden to, to pass letters.
And people probably wanted to say if you have this, you go to that and that address.
You know, it was dangerous.
My letters were--
For friends who asked.
You never asked names.
You know, if--
No, no.
And the Germans opened my suitcase, saw my letters, and they took me into some kind of interrogation thing.
I never was scared.
It's funny.
We cannot pass letters to our friends, so I do it because you forbid it.
He said, well, don't get caught again.
Not everybody will be as nice as I am.
And I went.
You know, I had things like that again and again, because I spoke German well.
Uh-uh.
No.
No.
Never--
No.
No, absolutely not.
It was one of the, the things we were told.
You don't talk about it.
Yeah, we-- well, we hold-- yeah, but we don't know who they were.
You know, they were sent.
Somebody said, I come from Charles.
Can I spend the night here?
Of course.
Spend the night.
The  next day, he goes.
Dozens of times.
But I don't--
Mostly no.
They were men, and they were miserable.
My father often gave them clothes, whatever clothes we had, because it was the war.
No.
I remember people who looked horribly sad and, and scared.
But they had to come you know, before curfew, leave after curfew, in broad daylight.
If you could have done that in the night, it  would have been easier.
But you couldn't, because you couldn't go out at night.
And that's why most of the people-- the Jews who were arrested, they were arrested at 4, 5 o'clock in the morning, when nobody was on the street.
You couldn't be out.
So we don't know.
We didn't know what was happening, because we couldn't see it.
We are in the house until 7:00 in the morning.
If you went out, you were arrested.
Did you have hiding places in your house?
Or were they just regular room?
No, it was an apartment.
That was the bad thing about it.
It was an apartment with a concierge, who watch everybody going in and out.
And the concierge was not a bad woman, but who knows?
You didn't trust anybody, you know.
Well, they came after our friend Schieck, Wolf Schieck told me they would come.
And we said oh no, nothing happened.
My father spoke fluent German, so did I.
And they apologized, and they left.
I mean, it was nothing, really, I mean two minutes.
No.
With their morale, with their food, with their identity papers, with contact.

Mostly, I would say, it considering my means, I was 19, with my, my moral help, with my going out with them, hiding their, their, you know, their stars, trying to find food for them, telling my friends to buy from them when they could.
And then comes the story of, of Rita.
No.
No, and if people were sick--
No.
Mostly, it was men.
And if they were sick, they didn't-- they didn't let on.
They had to get out.
No.
I know only that if they were--they needed something, my father gave them medicine.
That I know.
It was easy for him, he did that.
And he gave them names.
But--
Yes, I mean for safe houses along the way, that kind of thing.
Only people.
I didn't know where they were, nobody knew.
I mean, certainly, they would not involve a schoolgirl or a young girl in those things.
I mean, I was kept out.
I was only told to do little things where I wouldn't be suspected, and where I couldn't talk.
I knew nothing.
Even if they have caught me and tortured me, even if I had been the weakest of weakest, I didn't know anything.
Yeah.
I mean, warning in the [FRENCH] was, but not with us.
We were in a city.
And as I explained, how would you hide people in a house like this one?
You just can't.
You can for one night, two nights, but not for an extended time.
No.
I didn't know until 1942.
Because the BBC gave mainly military news, mainly battles.
And our-- all our attention-- of course, it doesn't sound good now-- was with the Russians.
Because we knew-- and I know, and I'm sure now-- that without the Russian, D-day would not have been possible.
The Russian-- the Russian took the brunt of everything.
That I saw it through my own eyes.
I saw the Germans we had in Dijon.
I knew which one-- I mean, people knew them.
They were living in houses.
None came back.
And the few that came on furlough, they were like ghosts.
They were half frozen, they were wild, they were-- and the worst thing for them was to be sent there.
To the Russian front.
In our building, we had the German official living on the fifth floor.
It had belonged to Jewish people, and the apartment had been taken by a colonel, a [FRENCH].
He was of high class.
And he had only one fear.
It was to be sent to the front, to Russia.
Occasionally, he spoke to me when he had problem with the laundry and the concierge and something and the orderly.
And it was his fear to be sent there.
As a matter of fact, his greatest wish was to be caught by the Americans when the Americans came.
He remained to the end of the war in our building, so he had high protections.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was-- I don't think he was a bad man.
He was-- I don't know what he was.
He was-- I had a funny story to tell you about that.
We had an elevator.
But it was forbidden to everybody except the German.
He had a key.
He was living on the fifth floor.
And me-- we had-- to heat our house, we had a little stove and wood that we went to get to the country.
And the wood we kept in the cellar.
And if you knew anything about French houses, the ceilings are as high as that.
And we lived on the fifth floor, which is like a good sixth floor in America, plus the floor to the cellar, which means seven floors.
And my mother didn't want the maid to go, the bad maid, because she felt she was an old woman.
It was up to us, the kids, to do it.
So I had a large basket and put logs in it.
And when I came to the ground floor in front of the elevator, there stood the German.
And he told me, you have to bring that to the fourth floor?
He said, why don't you go in the elevator with me?
Then you have just one floor to get down.
And I said yes.
So we went in the elevator, a bit like that.
You know, the French elevator-- put the basket of wood.
He stood, I stood.
Up we go.
The electric comes off.
We stay between two floors.
So he tells me, call.
I didn't want to call, because I didn't want anybody in the house to know I was with the German in the elevator.
So I didn't say anything.
And he pushed me.
He said, well, call for help.
And he said, help us, help us, you know, [GERMAN].
And the, the French-- oh, it's the German.
He's well where he is.
Let him be, he's fine.
But me, I didn't dare say anything.
So we remained a good while, until I heard my mother.
She said, Mado, are you here?
I said, yes, I'm in the elevator.
I'm with the German.
Oh, she said.
She said, all right.
She went to get the super, and he cranked us to the next floor.
[LAUGHS] And that's what my-- my big collaboration was.
[LAUGHS] It didn't turn out too well.
So anyway, I, I finished climbing the stairs with my wood.
But he was perfectly all right.
I mean, that's-- And at that time, Rita, Rita's mother was playing the piano.
And all of a sudden, she had a fit of madness.
She fell off.
In Dijon.
She came back.
Near us, in a little house, a little, tiny house with a garden, not far from our apartment house.
She fell on her back, and she started screaming.
And the doctor had to be called.
And she became-- she was insane.
And she had to be put in the asylum.
And the father of Rita gave money so she was, you know, reasonably well taken care of, as much as they knew how to take of in the '40s at a time of war.
But then the Germans came to arrest them, so they just had to run away.
So the father ran, and the, the-- Alfred and Rita.
Two of the teachers of the high school were able to go to the south.
And Rita came to my grandmother's house.
Near-- not too near Geneva, but not too far.
It's not Bellegarde, but a bit further.
But it was in France, not in Switzerland.
It was in France, in a house in the country, in a small village.
And we told my grandmother who Rita was.
And my grandmother-- Catholic grandmother-- didn't have any Jew under her roof, and she didn't want Rita.
She said Rita is going to sleep in the, in the barn, with the, with the rabbits.
We have plenty of hay.
She'll be safer.
If the police come, they will never dream.
So we said fine, my sister and I.
We took our pillows, and we were to sleep with the rabbits, too.
And then my grandfather came.
He was a good man, a bit weak.
And he said to my grandmother, no.
That's-- we are not going to happen in my roof.
Rita is going to have a bed, and she's going to have linen sheets and embroidered pillows.
And that's what happened.
So we all had a bed, and we all have our linen.
And Rita remained-- I don't remember, a week, two weeks.
I don't remember.
No, she went with the teachers.
We all went separately with false paper, with-- very difficult.
The teachers, the teachers.
No.
I had my papers, and my uncle was the mayor.
And it was-- still, he was not pursued yet.
He was still OK.
To go to see  my grandma.
It was vacation, it was summer.
And Rita came-- it was in September.
School starts in October there.
Pardon?
It's in the-- what did I do with my little-- my little pictures?
Oh, don't-- you can't get up.
I think it's '83.
No, no, no, '42.
Let me see, yes, '42.
And they both went-- succeeded through other means, not mine, to go to Switzerland.
And in Switzerland, they were not well treated by, by the family of her mother in Basel.
Not welcome.
They were not happy there.
Rita's grandmother.
She had been the wife of the grand rabbi.
And she had died, and the grand rabbi had remarried.
So Rita was with her grandmother-- not with her grandfather, but with her step grandmother.
And she was very unhappy there.
Bad times.
Yes, she survived the war.
And when she was at my grandmother-- the grandfather-- the father was caught.
And he became the-- he was-- he had the Iron Cross from the First World War.
He had been a captain.
But as I told you before, he had enlisted in the French army.
And he kept both on himself, which was silly.
So for the Germans, he was a traitor.
And Thalmann is not a Jewish name.
But then they discovered he was Jewish, you know, the usual means.
And he became the orderly of an SS who kicked him to death.
He died in Auschwitz, too.
Not very nice story, eh?
